paragraphs.text: The European Lotteries Association ("EL") welcomes the opportunity to contribute to the European Commission's public consultation on the future Digital Fairness Act ("DFA"). EL is the umbrella organization for state-owned and state-licensed lotteries in Europe that operate in the public interest. They provide their services within strictly regulated and responsible frameworks that prioritize consumer protection while generating substantial funding for society — supporting public health, education, culture, sport and other good causes. In 2024 alone, these contributions amounted to €24 billion. This unique "lottery-for-the-common-good" model distinguishes EL Members from purely commercial operators and underscores their role as safe, trusted and socially beneficial entertainment providers. Against this background, EL wishes to highlight several key considerations from the lotteries' perspective that we believe should be taken into account when designing the DFA.

section_path: The European Lotteries' Response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: Since 2018, one of the core guiding principles of the EU's digital policy has been that "what is illegal offline is also illegal online". In the Digital Compass for a Digital Decade from 2021, the Commission stressed that "digital transformation should also enable modern and efficient justice systems, enforcement of consumer rights and an increased effectiveness of public action including law enforcement and investigation capacities". The fight against illegal content online was then further enshrined in particular in Regulation 2022/2065 of 19 October 2022 on a Single Market for Digital Services ("DSA").

section_path: The European Lotteries' Response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: manipulative interface design (dark patterns)
misleading marketing by social media influencers
online profiling

paragraphs.text: Building on this, the DFA will now aim to "tackle unethical techniques and commercial practices related to manipulative interface design (dark patterns), misleading marketing by social media influencers, addictive design of digital products and online profiling, especially where consumer vulnerabilities are exploited for commercial purposes".

section_path: The European Lotteries' Response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: EL supports the goals of these initiatives and acknowledges the DFA as a step forward in protecting consumers online, especially vulnerable users and minors. However, many of these goals can already be achieved by better enforcing existing laws. Any new rules should be proportionate and respect national competences.

section_path: The European Lotteries' Response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: Gambling is recognized as a sector that requires tailored national regulation. The Conclusions of the Edinburgh European Council of 1992 and consistent case-law of the Court of Justice of the EU ("CJEU") explicitly confirm that Member States are best placed to regulate gambling according to their specific social, cultural and public health needs.

section_path: The DFA in the Context of Gambling

paragraphs.text: Page 1 of 6

section_path: The DFA in the Context of Gambling

evidence_quotes.quote: the exclusion of gambling from both the E-Commerce Directive and the Services Directive reinforces this approach, as the "home country control" rule does not apply to this sector.

the exclusion of "gambling activities" stems from the fact that the gambling sector is not harmonized and that the CJEU has clarified that there is no mutual recognition of authorizations in the sector (Markus Stoß C-316/07, para. 112).

paragraphs.text: Consumer protection and public order are the two fundamental pillars of any national gambling regulation. The exclusion of gambling from both the E-Commerce Directive and the Services Directive reinforces this approach, as the "home country control" rule does not apply to this sector. Indeed, the exclusion of "gambling activities" stems from the fact that the gambling sector is not harmonized and that the CJEU has clarified that there is no mutual recognition of authorizations in the sector (Markus Stoß C-316/07, para. 112).

section_path: The DFA in the Context of Gambling

paragraphs.text: The DFA should respect the subsidiarity principle and back any existing national frameworks to tackle harmful digital practices. In this regard, EL would favour guidelines, clarification and simplification of existing rules and better implementation of them, over new binding legislation.

section_path: The DFA in the Context of Gambling

evidence_quotes.quote: regulated online gambling operated by lotteries

paragraphs.text: EL underlines the necessity for the DFA to take into consideration the special nature of regulated online gambling operated by lotteries that have consumer protection at their very core.

section_path: Tackling Illegal Gambling Operators to Protect Consumers Online

evidence_quotes.quote: online gambling carries higher risks than offline gambling.

paragraphs.text: It is recognized that online games of chance pose different and more substantial risks of fraud by operators against consumers compared to the traditional – offline – markets, largely due to the lack of direct contact between the consumer and the operator. At the WTO level, analyses have also confirmed that online gambling carries higher risks than offline gambling.

section_path: Tackling Illegal Gambling Operators to Protect Consumers Online

evidence_quotes.quote: online lottery tickets being different than other online games of chance or gambling.

paragraphs.text: Further to this, it is important to assess the specific risks associated with the type of online games – online lottery tickets being different than other online games of chance or gambling.

section_path: Tackling Illegal Gambling Operators to Protect Consumers Online

evidence_quotes.quote: manipulative design features
target vulnerable groups, including minors and self-excluded players

paragraphs.text: The main threat to consumer safety stems from illegal online gambling operators who provide services without the required national licenses in the country of the consumer. These operators evade taxation and anti-money laundering obligations, deploy manipulative design features and deceptive advertising and frequently target vulnerable groups, including minors and self-excluded players (called the strategy of vulnerability victimization). Such practices create risks of addiction, fraud and social harm.

section_path: Tackling Illegal Gambling Operators to Protect Consumers Online

evidence_quotes.quote: to verify the licensing status of gambling operators and their advertisers.
introducing clear verification indicators to distinguish licensed operators from unlicensed ones,

items.topic: Removal Of Illegal Gambling Content And Advertising

evidence_quotes.quote: requiring the swift removal of illegal gambling content and advertising flagged by regulators or trusted flaggers.

items.topic: Tools To Disable Illegal Gambling Infrastructure

evidence_quotes.quote: national authorities should be provided with effective and innovative tools to disable the infrastructure of illegal operators.

paragraphs.text: EL is of the opinion that the DFA should complement the DSA by compelling online platforms to verify the licensing status of gambling operators and their advertisers. This could include introducing clear verification indicators to distinguish licensed operators from unlicensed ones, as well as requiring the swift removal of illegal gambling content and advertising flagged by regulators or trusted flaggers. In addition, national authorities should be provided with effective and innovative tools to disable the infrastructure of illegal operators.

section_path: Tackling Illegal Gambling Operators to Protect Consumers Online

paragraphs.text: Beyond the technical aspects used by illegal gambling operators to cheat players, respect for the rule of the law should be enforced against Member States that ignore consumer's rights as we have seen in recent consumer cases about reimbursement claims introduced by German and Austrian consumers for losses incurred with online gambling operators based and licensed in Malta. These cases highlight how illegal gambling affects consumers across the EU.

section_path: Tackling Illegal Gambling Operators to Protect Consumers Online

paragraphs.text: The UN Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC) estimates that illegal gambling – mostly illicit betting markets – amounts to approximately USD 1,700 billion per year, covering bets placed in unauthorized,

section_path: Tackling Illegal Gambling Operators to Protect Consumers Online

paragraphs.text: Page 2 of 6

section_path: Tackling Illegal Gambling Operators to Protect Consumers Online

evidence_quotes.quote: illegal gambling does not benefit from consumer protection

paragraphs.text: unregulated or illicit markets. Under EU law, illegal gambling does not benefit from consumer protection, as confirmed by the CJEU. This was further noted in a recent Opinion at the CJEU, which found that no abuse of EU law is established when a consumer claims for reimbursement of illegal online games losses played in another Member State.

section_path: Tackling Illegal Gambling Operators to Protect Consumers Online

evidence_quotes.quote: the theory of controlled expansion as a means to protect consumers in the gambling sector.

paragraphs.text: Finally, the CJEU acknowledged the theory of controlled expansion as a means to protect consumers in the gambling sector. Expanding games may be justified where illegal activities are significant in scale and regarding the circumstances. In this context, the aggressive advertising practices of private actors promoting illegal gambling content – as well as their use of new media on internet platforms – must be taken into account. EL notes the emergence of such “private advertisers in favor of illegal activities”, in some Member States, and stresses the need to ensure that these advertisers and third-party platforms comply with EU digital consumer rules.

section_path: Tackling Illegal Gambling Operators to Protect Consumers Online

evidence_quotes.quote: consumer protection in gambling
The DFA should support strong consumer protection through Member States’ regulations on gambling.

paragraphs.text: Taken together, these developments demonstrate the intersection of digital regulation and consumer protection in gambling. The DFA should support strong consumer protection through Member States’ regulations on gambling. At the same time, there is also a responsibility of the European Commission to ensure that digital rules – in particular the DSA and the upcoming DFA – are effectively applied in specific sectors such as gambling.

section_path: Tackling Illegal Gambling Operators to Protect Consumers Online

evidence_quotes.quote: the sale of metadata to third parties.

paragraphs.text: EL has also observed that dominant intermediaries (VLOPs and VLOSEs) often engage in business models that go beyond simple hosting, including commercial agreements and the sale of metadata to third parties. Such activities cannot be exempted under Article 14 of the E-Commerce Directive.

section_path: Tackling Illegal Gambling Operators to Protect Consumers Online

paragraphs.text: EL therefore reiterates the responsibility of large platforms to act responsibly in this context.

section_path: Tackling Illegal Gambling Operators to Protect Consumers Online

evidence_quotes.quote: host or market gambling content
dominant online platforms (VLOPs and VLOSEs) – that host or market gambling content

paragraphs.text: All actors in the digital environment bear responsibility. In the gambling context, this means both operators of gambling services and intermediary services – in particular dominant online platforms (VLOPs and VLOSEs) – that host or market gambling content (e.g., Google, Apple, Meta).

section_path: Tackling Illegal Gambling Operators to Protect Consumers Online
Responsibility of Intermediary Services

paragraphs.text: While gambling is excluded from the E-Commerce Directive as a service, the responsibility of online platforms – whether as intermediaries or hosts – still applies in the context of gambling. The DSA also explicitly acknowledges Member States’ competence in addressing illegal gambling content (Recitals 31–32) but does not exempt intermediaries from liability for such content. Accordingly, intermediary services remain responsible for promoting gambling, including illegal gambling websites, applications featuring gambling activities or any gambling-related content that is not the service itself. Effective consumer protection requires clear rules and robust enforcement at both national and EU levels, with dominant platforms actively participating in efforts to fight illegal gambling.

section_path: Tackling Illegal Gambling Operators to Protect Consumers Online
Responsibility of Intermediary Services

evidence_quotes.quote: Online Interface Design and Organisation

paragraphs.text: Furthermore, EL welcomes the key role that the DSA plays in supporting these protections. In particular Articles 9 (“Orders to Act against Illegal Content”), 16 (“Notice and Action Mechanism”), 25 (“Online Interface Design and Organisation”), 26 (“Advertising on Online Platforms”) and 28 (“Online Protection

evidence_quotes.quote: the age-verification tool.
protections for minors
the European Digital Identity Wallet

paragraphs.text: of Minors"). EL also supports the Guidance for Platforms under Article 28, the Guidelines on the Protection of Minors Online and the age-verification tool. Given the specific risks that online gambling poses to consumers — especially minors — EL is committed to ensuring that access to gambling content complies fully with EU digital rules. The effectiveness of measures such as the European Digital Identity Wallet and protections for minors, however, will depend on all operators adhering to the rules consistently.

section_path: Tackling Illegal Gambling Operators to Protect Consumers Online
Responsibility of Intermediary Services

evidence_quotes.quote: The DFA expressly refers to loot boxes as digital products that require more transparency concerning the odds of winning when buying virtual items with uncertainty-based rewards.

paragraphs.text: The DFA expressly refers to loot boxes as digital products that require more transparency concerning the odds of winning when buying virtual items with uncertainty-based rewards. The contemporary digital environment also exposes consumers to new risks such as in-game purchases and loot boxes. While loot boxes are not considered gambling unless items can be sold for real money, they nevertheless raise consumer protection concerns. These are best addressed with gaming-specific safeguards, without extending gambling legislation. Game publishers should design such features responsibly, with the protection of minors as a priority.

section_path: Tackling Illegal Gambling Operators to Protect Consumers Online
Addressing Wider Digital Risks: Loot Boxes

evidence_quotes.quote: loot boxes
in-game purchasing systems

paragraphs.text: Globally, several jurisdictions have intervened to control loot boxes, some applying gambling laws and others implementing more general rules framed as "consumer protection". A study by the European Parliament recommended that loot boxes and in-game purchasing systems be regulated from a "consumer protection" perspective. The Digital Fitness Check of October 2024 by the European Commission also acknowledges that digital addiction — including in connection with loot boxes — poses a threat to the most vulnerable consumers.

section_path: Tackling Illegal Gambling Operators to Protect Consumers Online
Addressing Wider Digital Risks: Loot Boxes

evidence_quotes.quote: loot box mechanisms
banning loot boxes

paragraphs.text: The European Commission suggests more transparency regarding loot box mechanisms or even the default disabling of in-game payments and loot boxes. IMCO also calls on the Commission to ensure that the forthcoming DFA provides strong protection for minors who play video games, particularly by banning loot boxes, as well as other forms of randomized content offered in exchange for real money in games accessible to minors.

section_path: Tackling Illegal Gambling Operators to Protect Consumers Online
Addressing Wider Digital Risks: Loot Boxes

evidence_quotes.quote: loot boxes

paragraphs.text: EL supports these developments and advocates for a national-level definition of what constitutes gambling, including determining whether loot boxes should be governed by gambling laws or consumer protection rules.

section_path: Tackling Illegal Gambling Operators to Protect Consumers Online
Addressing Wider Digital Risks: Loot Boxes

evidence_quotes.quote: Loot boxes in the App Store in Belgium

paragraphs.text: EL also notes that a preliminary ruling in the Apple Distribution case (Loot boxes in the App Store in Belgium) was withdrawn after Apple settled the case out of Court with the consumer protection public authorities. It illustrates that digital platforms can bear responsibility regarding access to loot boxes.

section_path: Tackling Illegal Gambling Operators to Protect Consumers Online
Addressing Wider Digital Risks: Loot Boxes

evidence_quotes.quote: manipulative design practices
aggressive digital advertising practices — particularly via social media, influencers and through fake reviews

paragraphs.text: At the same time, aggressive digital advertising practices — particularly via social media, influencers and through fake reviews — allow unlicensed operators to reach consumers outside regulated systems. Lotteries, by contrast, operate under strict rules that prohibit manipulative design practices and ensure close national supervision, including pre-launch product reviews, advertising codes and advanced player protection systems. The DFA should help close enforcement gaps while avoiding unnecessary EU-level harmonization in sectors that are already highly regulated.

section_path: Tackling Illegal Gambling Operators to Protect Consumers Online
Addressing Wider Digital Risks: Loot Boxes

paragraphs.text: Page 4 of 6

section_path: Tackling Illegal Gambling Operators to Protect Consumers Online
Addressing Wider Digital Risks: Loot Boxes

paragraphs.text: The digital world in which minors are immersed today represents increased risks. Some research points to Generation Z and Generation Alpha as experiencing heightened mental health and social challenges, due in particular to the extensive digital environment and certain platform practices. These generations face issues related to technological change and a sense of isolation while being constantly connected through multiple devices and platforms.

section_path: Protecting Minors Online

evidence_quotes.quote: Licensed lotteries provide some of the strongest consumer protections in Europe.
They operate under strict national rules, including robust age verification and player identification checks, spending limits, self-exclusion schemes and extensive monitoring obligations.

paragraphs.text: Licensed lotteries provide some of the strongest consumer protections in Europe. They operate under strict national rules, including robust age verification and player identification checks, spending limits, self-exclusion schemes and extensive monitoring obligations. Advertising and digital offerings are closely supervised by national authorities and lotteries are independently certified under internationally recognized responsible gaming standards, including EL's own framework. These measures ensure safe play, transparency in odds and risks and sustainable operations that directly benefit society.

section_path: Protecting Minors Online

paragraphs.text: The responsibility and accountability of digital platforms in protecting minors is central to ensuring EU values, trust and safety online.

section_path: Protecting Minors Online

evidence_quotes.quote: gambling

paragraphs.text: The impact of gambling on health and social care can be significant, although legal gambling offers substantial benefits when properly managed. Digital platforms should also take responsibility, especially for illegal activities, which are the most harmful to consumers.

section_path: Protecting Minors Online

evidence_quotes.quote: should remain proportionate and targeted.
Operators already subject to demanding national rules should not face redundant obligations or additional administrative burdens.
Simplification and proportionality should be guiding principles for the DFA.

paragraphs.text: Therefore, EL emphasizes that future EU measures – including the DFA – should remain proportionate and targeted. Operators already subject to demanding national rules should not face redundant obligations or additional administrative burdens. Simplification and proportionality should be guiding principles for the DFA.

section_path: Protecting Minors Online

evidence_quotes.quote: tackling manipulative digital practices

paragraphs.text: EL supports the Commission's objectives of strengthening enforcement, improving transparency and tackling manipulative digital practices, which are essential to protect consumers in the online environment while at the same time, gambling regulation must remain exclusively a national competence.

section_path: Protecting Minors Online

evidence_quotes.quote: illegal gambling websites
unlicensed operators

paragraphs.text: Lotteries already provide a high level of consumer protection and play a unique societal role under the "lottery-for-the-common-good" model. The upcoming DFA presents an important opportunity to further strengthen consumer protection in the digital environment while ensuring coherence with existing frameworks and national responsibilities. EL calls for measures that give Member States stronger tools to act against illegal gambling websites and advertising, ensure platform accountability to prevent unlicensed operators from reaching EU consumers and reinforce consumer protection while respecting national competence.

section_path: Conclusion

evidence_quotes.quote: in the context of gambling

paragraphs.text: EL also emphasizes the need for strong enforcement of the DFA, alongside existing rules – in particular the DSA – in the context of gambling and highlights the responsibility of digital platforms (VLOPs and VLOSEs) in fighting illegal operators.

section_path: Conclusion

paragraphs.text: EL and its Members stand ready to cooperate with the European Commission to ensure that the DFA delivers tangible benefits for consumers, protects the integrity of regulated markets and sustains the vital contributions of national lotteries to society.

paragraphs.text: THE EUROPEAN LOTTERIES

section_path: Conclusion

paragraphs.text: FOR THE BENEFIT OF SOCIETY

section_path: Conclusion

evidence_quotes.quote: games of chance, including sports betting.

paragraphs.text: The European Lotteries Association ("EL") is the European umbrella organization of entities operating national lotteries and other games of chance, including sports betting. The Members of EL are operating their services for the public benefit. EL is the largest and most representative lottery and gambling sector's organization in Europe, present in 39 European countries with a total of 70 Members, including all EU Member States (50 Members are in the EU and 20 not). EL stands for the sound and sustainable activity model for the benefit of society, based on the values of forward-thinking, responsibility and integrity. EL Members only operate in those jurisdictions in which they are licensed by the state and where they comply with the legal requirements (e.g. anti-money laundering, responsible gaming policies, etc.) and act as the most responsible operators in each jurisdiction. It is estimated that around half of the European adult population annually participates in the games that are offered by the EL Members.

section_path: About The European Lotteries Association

evidence_quotes.quote: games of chance.

paragraphs.text: In 2024, EL Members contributed €24 billion to good causes and societal initiatives, reinforcing their core mission: generating funds for public benefit through safe and responsible games of chance.
